title: Structural glasses model using disorder fields: the boson peak from local ground states
authors: M. M. Balbino, I. P. de Freitas, A. M. S. Macedo, G. Krein, N. F. Svaiter
abstract: We show the emergence of a contribution characteristic of the boson peak in the spectral density of structural glasses. To model the vitreous state, we consider static density-fluctuation fields coupled to a multiplicative quenched disorder. Performing an ensemble average over all disorder realizations, a functional series representation of the average free energy is obtained. In this series representation of the average free energy for the glassy state of matter, we identify in the function space effective actions. These effective actions present a large number of metastable states and ground states. Random first-order transition, widely discussed in the literature as a description of the transition from the supercooled liquid to the glassy state of matter, emerges naturally in our formalism. We establish the connection between the use of hyperbolic differential equations with random coefficients and the presence of many ground states in the average free energy. This connection allows us to study emergent excitations in such amorphous materials.
summary: This theoretical work models structural glasses by treating them as systems with quenched disorder within a field-theoretic framework. It derives the random first-order transition from the free energy landscape and successfully predicts the characteristic boson peak in the vibrational density of states. This connects the structural complexity of amorphous materials to their measurable low-frequency excitation spectrum.
main_problem: The paper aims to theoretically explain the emergence of the boson peak in the vibrational density of states of structural glasses and to characterize the non-ergodic glassy phase.
main_result: The formalism naturally yields a description of the random first-order transition, establishing a link between the multiplicity of local ground states and the characteristic $\omega^4$ excess contribution to the spectral density (the boson peak).
method: The authors employ a functional field-theoretic approach, utilizing ensemble averaging over quenched disorder realizations and analyzing the resulting free energy landscape via replica methods and zeta-function formalisms.
model_or_system: The system is structural glasses (amorphous solids) modeled using a coarse-grained density field coupled to a multiplicative quenched disorder field. The analysis is performed in the context of continuum field theory.
key_observables: Average free energy functional, effective actions, spectral density $g(\omega)$, and the boson peak ($\delta g(\omega) \propto \omega^4$).
important_parameters: Disorder strength (covariance $C$), temperature/inverse temperature ($ eta$), and the dimensionality of the system ($d$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on a coarse-grained description, the use of the replica trick, and approximations such as the diagonal approximation in function space for simplifying the disorder average.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the functional framework for the average free energy in the presence of quenched disorder, to analyzing the resulting multivalley structure that characterizes the random first-order transition, and finally applying this to the dynamic problem to derive the low-frequency spectral density exhibiting the boson peak.
why_interesting: The connection drawn between the complex, multi-valley free energy landscape (a hallmark of disordered systems) and a specific, measurable excitation spectrum (the boson peak) provides a powerful theoretical link between static structural disorder and low-energy dynamics, relevant for understanding amorphous materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
